name: Nidhogg
description: Nidhogg, whose name translates roughly to "Malice Striker" or "Corpse-Eater," is a fearsome dragon or serpent in Norse mythology, primarily associated with gnawing at the roots of Yggdrasil, the World Tree. This act is not merely destructive but signifies the constant decay and struggle that underlies the cosmic order. Nidhogg resides in Niflheimr, the realm of primordial ice and darkness, specifically beneath one of Yggdrasil's three roots. The dragon's incessant gnawing contributes to the eventual collapse of the cosmos during Ragnarok. Further emphasizing Nidhogg's malevolent nature, he carries the corpses of oathbreakers, murderers, and adulterers to Náströnd, a shore of corpses in Hel, where he torments them. He is a symbol of destruction, primal chaos, and the consequences of moral transgression within the Norse worldview, constantly working to unravel the fabric of reality.
subtitle: The Corpse-Eating Dragon of Norse Mythology
appearance: Nidhogg is depicted as a large, serpentine dragon with dark scales and sharp claws. He is often described as having a fearsome visage, reflecting his malevolent nature. His presence is associated with decay and death, and his form embodies the destructive forces of the underworld.
habitat: Niflheimr, beneath the roots of Yggdrasil.
behavior: Nidhogg is characterized by his relentless gnawing at the roots of Yggdrasil, symbolizing the constant decay and struggle within the Norse cosmos. He also carries the corpses of the wicked to Náströnd, where he subjects them to eternal torment. His behavior is driven by malice and a desire to undermine the cosmic order.

keyMyths.title: Nidhogg and Yggdrasil
keyMyths.description: Nidhogg constantly gnaws at the roots of Yggdrasil, the World Tree, threatening its stability. This action is part of a larger cosmic struggle between order and chaos. The squirrels Ratatoskr runs up and down Yggdrasil carrying messages between Nidhogg and the eagle at the top, which causes conflict.

keyMyths.title: Nidhogg and Náströnd
keyMyths.description: Nidhogg carries the corpses of oathbreakers, murderers, and adulterers to Náströnd, a shore of corpses in Hel, where he torments them. This exemplifies his role as a punisher of the wicked and a harbinger of eternal suffering. He is an integral part of the afterlife as perceived by the Norse.

keyMyths.title: Nidhogg and Ragnarok
keyMyths.description: Nidhogg's actions contribute to the eventual collapse of the cosmos during Ragnarok. His gnawing weakens Yggdrasil, and his association with death and decay foreshadows the destruction that will engulf the world. He will fly from Niflheimr when Ragnarok begins.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A key source for Norse mythology, providing detailed accounts of Nidhogg's role in the cosmos and his association with Niflheimr and Náströnd.
sources.text: A key source for Norse mythology, providing detailed accounts of Nidhogg's role in the cosmos and his association with Niflheimr and Náströnd.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems that offer valuable insights into Norse mythology, including depictions of Nidhogg's gnawing at Yggdrasil's roots.
sources.text: A collection of Old Norse poems that offer valuable insights into Norse mythology, including depictions of Nidhogg's gnawing at Yggdrasil's roots.

sources.author: Hilda Ellis Davidson
sources.work: Gods and Myths of Northern Europe
sources.description: A scholarly analysis of Norse mythology, providing interpretations of Nidhogg's symbolism and significance within the broader context of Norse cosmology.
